macro_segments.title: Introduction and the Mishna on Nezirut with Failed Heter from Chacham

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context of the Mishna on Lamed-Aleph Amud-Bet
The case of someone who took a vow of Nezirut and asked a Chacham for Heter but was refused
Beit Shammai's position on Nezirut B'Taaut and the requirement for proper formula
The Mishna's teaching that one counts from the time of accepting Nezirut, even with violations

macro_segments.title: The Case of Retroactive Release of Nezirut and Sanctified Animal

micro_segments.title: When Chacham permits the Nezirut, the previously sanctified animal goes free
Beit Hillel's argument against Beit Shammai using the principle of Hekdesh Ta'us
Beit Shammai's counter-argument from Ma'aser Behema where error still creates sanctity
Beit Hillel's response: Ma'aser Behema works differently, not through Hekdesh Ta'us principle
Tosafot's explanation of why Beit Shammai doesn't respond and the principle of missing basic criteria

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis of the Mishna: Disputes over Making Up Violated Days

micro_segments.title: The Gemara assumes the Mishna discusses a case where one violated Nezirut by drinking wine
Dispute between Rabbi Yossi and Rabbanan on whether to make up violated days
Gemara's explanation: the Mishna can fit either Rabbi Yossi or Rabbanan depending on short vs. long Nezirut

macro_segments.title: The Case of Stolen Animal: Nezirut B'Taaut Based on Missing Prerequisite

micro_segments.title: Someone accepted Nezirut but found his animal was stolen
The Mishna distinguishes: if stolen before vow, he is Nazir; if stolen before he knew, he is not

macro_segments.title: Nachum HaMedai Permits Nezirim Based on Future Destruction; Chachamim Overturn

micro_segments.title: Principle of Ein Potchin B'Nolad: cannot open for something not yet born or known

macro_segments.title: The Case of Stolen Animal: Nezirut B'Taaut Based on Missing Prerequisite (continued)

micro_segments.title: Introduction of Nachum HaMedai's ruling on Nezirim returning from exile after Temple destruction

macro_segments.title: Nachum HaMedai Permits Nezirim Based on Future Destruction; Chachamim Overturn (continued)

micro_segments.title: Nachum HaMedai's reasoning: Had you known the Temple would be destroyed, would you have accepted Nezirut?
Chachamim's response: They distinguish based on when the Temple was destroyed versus when vow was accepted

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion: Rabbi Eliezer's View on Potchin B'Nolad and Rav Yosef's Alternative

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer holds Potchin B'Nolad; Chachamim overwhelm and silence him
Rava introduces Potchin B'Tnai Nolad: conditional argument about what would happen had people advised them
Rosh's explanation: the condition depends on listening to people's advice, not the objective future
Rav Yosef's alternative: appeal to the prophetic warning in Yirmiyahu about Temple destruction
Abaye's objection: even if they should have known, they could not have known exactly when

macro_segments.title: Case of Six People Meeting Unknown Person: Beit Shammai, Beit Hillel, Rabbi Tarfon, and Rabbi Shimon

micro_segments.title: Setup: Six people make conditional vows of Nezirut based on whether the approaching person is Reuven
Beit Shammai's position: All six become Nezirim because they intended Nezirut despite mistaken conditions
Beit Hillel's position: Only those whose conditions are met are Nezirim
Rabbi Tarfon's position: None become Nezirim because the condition is unclear at time of vow
Case where person turns away: Under Beit Hillel, none become Nezirim due to unmet condition
Rabbi Shimon's position: Safek Nazir who must accept optional Nezirut to conclude his status

topical_tags.term: Ein Potchin B'Nolad (Cannot open for future contingency)
Potchin B'Tnai Nolad (Opening with conditional future)
